title: A Look at Deck Box Dungeons
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B9FbWxNdJPY

Daniel (@dungeondive) is surprised by *Deck Box Dungeons* — a single-deck-of-cards small-box crawl that uses a free companion app to do most of what bigger games need hundreds of components for. He calls the app integration the best he's seen at this scale.

Character build is layered: pick a base hero card (Oathsworn, Wanderer, etc.), flip in a class (Elemental Mage, Berserker, Temporal Mage, Assassin, Monk, Wave Finder, ...) for ability cards and stat bonuses, then equip a weapon for melee/ranged/defence modifiers. Slide-in tracker cards manage health, energy, and treasure as resources accumulate.

The standout mechanism is enemy resolution. Spawning a "green melee" tells you nothing — a die roll picks which of the green-melee enemies (Slip Thief, Cultist) it actually is. Combat is custom d6 dice (sixes = hits, plus weapon bonuses), with defenders rolling to block. Energy banks as you hit, fuelling abilities later.

The app does the heavy lifting: spawns, random encounters, story prompts, ambient sound, multiple named quests (Troll Trial, Chest Full of Gold, hero-specific yarns) plus an exploration mode and PDF fall-backs for the app-averse. Treasure deck is intentionally lean and utility-focused; Daniel wants a booster pack.

----

*Deck Box Dungeons* leans heavily on an app to feel complete. Does that count as elegant offloading, or as a long-term liability when the app eventually stops being maintained?
